title: MokA: Multimodal Low-Rank Adaptation for MLLMs

reviews.summary: The paper proposes MoKA, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for multimodal large language models that explicitly separates unimodal adaptation from cross-modal adaptation. MoKA uses modality-specific low-rank A matrices to compress per-modality information, introduces a lightweight cross-attention between non-text queries and text keys/values in the low-rank space to emphasize task-relevant cross-modal cues, and employs a shared B matrix to project all modalities into a unified space. Across audio-visual-text, visual-text, and speech-text settings and multiple LLM backbones (LLaMA2/3, Qwen2/2.5-VL), MoKA shows consistent performance gains over LoRA and several strong LoRA variants, with modest parameter and latency overhead.
strengths: The paper articulates a clear multimodal-aware design principle: decoupling unimodal compression (per-modality A) from explicit cross-modal interaction, then reuniting modality streams via a shared B. This reconceptualizes the roles of A/B in LoRA specifically for MLLMs.
Introducing cross-attention in the low-rank space is a neat idea that adds interaction capacity with minimal overhead and a clean integration into the LoRA pathway.
The partial modality inference analysis is insightful, surfacing a non-obvious failure mode of shared-parameter LoRA in multimodal settings (text dominance) and motivating the design.
Extensive evaluation across three distinct modality combinations (audio-visual-text, visual-text, speech-text) and four backbones demonstrates robustness and breadth.
Comparisons cover a range of LoRA variants (Multiple LoRA, LoRAMoE, DoRA, HydraLoRA, Uni-modal LoRA) and additional multimodal-aware baselines proposed by the authors.
Ablations on cross-attention and interaction variants support the claim that explicit cross-modal interaction is beneficial beyond a single module instantiation.
Efficiency analysis reports trainable parameter ratios and inference latency overheads.
The motivation, architecture, and mathematical description are clear and consistent. Figures effectively communicate the differences between standard LoRA and MoKA.
The role of each component (A per modality, cross-attention, shared B) is well-explained, and equations match the narrative design.
The work addresses a widely relevant problem—PEFT for MLLMs—and proposes a simple, practical modification that consistently yields measurable gains.
The modality-aware perspective and low-rank cross-attention have potential to influence future PEFT designs for multimodal setups.
weaknesses: The novelty is moderate: modality-specific adapters and cross-attention between modalities are established concepts; the main contribution is an effective arrangement within a LoRA-like pathway rather than a fundamentally new mechanism.
The omission of Wq/Wk/Wv projections in cross-attention may limit flexibility; while justified by A acting as projections, this design choice may under-explore richer attention parameterizations.

Missing details about injection points (which layers and which weight matrices receive MoKA—Q/K/V/O/MLP?), rank allocation per site, and whether placement differs across backbones. This affects replicability and fairness of comparisons.
No variance or multi-seed reporting; improvements, while consistent, are often modest (1–2 points) and would benefit from statistical robustness analysis.
Hyperparameters for cross-attention strength (λa, λv), attention heads (if any), and their tuning ranges are not specified; sensitivity analyses are missing.
Efficiency reporting is limited (single hardware/config unspecified); memory footprint and training-time overheads are not fully characterized.
Some artifacts in tables/labels (e.g., emojis/text placeholders) and minor inconsistencies in method naming could be cleaned up for a camera-ready version.

The paper could better position itself against modality-specific adapters or connector architectures in MLLMs that also use cross-attention inside LLM layers (e.g., modular cross-attention connectors, adapter fusion or composition schemes).
No exploration of alternative PEFT baselines beyond LoRA variants, such as prompt tuning or adapter fusion approaches tailored to multimodal interactions.
reviews.questions:
1. Where exactly is MoKA injected in the LLM layers? Which weight matrices (Q, K, V, O, MLP up/down) receive the MoKA update and does the placement differ across backbones and scenarios?
2. What values do you use for λa and λv, and how sensitive are the results to these hyperparameters and to the rank r? Please include a sensitivity analysis.
3. Did you try including lightweight Wq/Wk/Wv projections separate from A to allow multi-head attention or larger attention capacity within the low-rank space? If so, how does performance/overhead change?
4. How many runs per experiment were conducted, and what is the variance across seeds? Are the gains statistically significant on key benchmarks?
5. Can you report full efficiency metrics (training time, memory usage) and hardware details, and compare MoKA vs LoRA under identical settings?
6. For Qwen2.5-VL, could you enable training of the visual connector (or use a comparable external projector) to provide a fairer comparison across backbones?
7. How does performance vary with different layer subsets for MoKA insertion (e.g., attention-only vs MLP-only vs both) and with different ranks per layer?
reviews.assessment: This paper offers a clear and compelling motivation for multimodal-aware PEFT and presents a simple, well-integrated design—per-modality low-rank compression plus low-rank cross-attention and a shared projection—that consistently improves over standard LoRA and several strong variants across diverse multimodal scenarios and backbones. The partial-modality inference analysis is insightful and strengthens the case for explicitly balancing unimodal and cross-modal adaptation. While the conceptual novelty is moderate (building on known ideas of modality-specific adapters and cross-attention), the specific arrangement within a LoRA-style pathway is practically valuable and yields robust empirical gains with modest overhead. The main weaknesses are missing implementation details (injection points, seeds, sensitivity), limited statistical analysis, and incomplete positioning relative to broader multimodal adapter/fusion literature. With these clarifications and minor additions, I believe the work would make a useful contribution to the NeurIPS community. I lean toward a weak accept.
